perturbation.original_text: Holdings agrees that it will keep the Information confidential and that the Information will not, without the prior written consent of Big Sky, be disclosed by Holdings or by its officers, directors, employees, agents or representatives (collectively, "Representatives"), in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, and shall not be used by Holdings or by its Representatives, other than in connection with evaluating the Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Holdings agrees to endeavor to keep the Information confidential and that the Information will not, without what Holdings considers to be reasonable prior consultation with Big Sky, be disclosed by Holdings or by its officers, directors, employees, agents or representatives (collectively, "Representatives"), in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, and should primarily be used by Holdings or by its Representatives, other than in connection with evaluating the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces "will keep" with "endeavor to keep," weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality. "Without the prior written consent" is replaced with "without what Holdings considers to be reasonable prior consultation," making the requirement subjective and dependent on Holdings' interpretation. Changing "shall not be used" to "should primarily be used" further weakens the obligation, suggesting that using the information for other purposes is possible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 - 325C.09 Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (§ 325C.02) protects trade secrets, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text aligns with this by mandating confidentiality. The modified text weakens this obligation, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the Act if Holdings' 'endeavor' and 'reasonable consultation' are deemed insufficient to protect the confidentiality, thus violating the Act's intent to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated confidentiality, aligning with the intent of Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act to protect trade secrets by requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified text weakens this obligation by changing "will keep" to "endeavor to keep," "prior written consent" to "what Holdings considers to be reasonable prior consultation," and "shall not be used" to "should primarily be used." These changes introduce ambiguity and subjectivity, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard as described in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1, Sec. 325C.02, discusses injunctive relief for misappropriation, implying a legal standard for protecting trade secrets that the modified text could undermine, as the weakened confidentiality terms could be seen as insufficient protection. This creates a significant contradiction because the modified terms could be interpreted as not fulfilling the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required to protect trade secrets, therefore a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: During such time as the parties are involved in discussions or negotiations concerning the Transaction and for a period of at least 60 days after conclusion thereof, neither party shall engage in any transactions whatsoever regarding the common stock of the other, whether publicly traded or privately held, registered or nonregistered, including, but not limited to, buying, selling, taking or granting options in or making or receiving offers or tender offers to buy, sell, trade, or exchange any stock of the other, without the express written consent of the other and without first fully complying with all applicable securities laws and regulations and the rules of all exchanges upon which the stock of each respective party is traded, provided however, that the foregoing shall not be construed to limit the right of either party to invest in mutual funds or other commercially available investment vehicles that may acquire the stock of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: During such time as the parties are involved in discussions or negotiations concerning the Transaction and for a period of approximately 60 days after conclusion thereof, neither party shall generally engage in transactions regarding the common stock of the other, whether publicly traded or privately held, registered or nonregistered, including, but not limited to, buying, selling, taking or granting options in or making or receiving offers or tender offers to buy, sell, trade, or exchange any stock of the other, without making good faith efforts to comply with applicable securities laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing "at least 60 days" with "approximately 60 days," making the duration of the restriction uncertain. Additionally, the phrase "shall engage in any transactions whatsoever" is weakened to "shall generally engage in transactions", suggesting that some transactions may be acceptable. Also, "without first fully complying" is replaced with "without making good faith efforts to comply," which introduces a subjective standard of compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text aimed to prevent insider trading by explicitly forbidding transactions without full compliance with securities laws. The modified text, by using 'good faith efforts,' allows for potential loopholes and arguments that a party tried to comply but failed, thus creating a situation where insider trading could occur without clear violation of the agreement, contradicting the purpose of the Securities Exchange Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices related to securities. The original text ensures compliance with securities laws by stating "without first fully complying". The modified text replaces this with "without making good faith efforts to comply," which allows for potential non-compliance, undermining the original intent to prevent insider trading. The phrase 'approximately 60 days' also weakens the enforceability of the original time frame of 'at least 60 days'. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, explicitly states it is unlawful "(a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud" or "(c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." By introducing the concept of 'good faith efforts,' the changed text opens the door for actions that could be construed as fraudulent or deceptive, thereby contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota applicable to contracts between residents of Minnesota that are to be wholly performed within such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally guided by the laws of the State of Minnesota, while giving some consideration to principles of equity and fairness, and also generally considering the laws applicable to contracts between residents of Minnesota.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that Minnesota law governs the agreement. The modified text replaces 'governed by and construed in accordance with' with 'generally guided by,' weakening the binding nature of Minnesota law. The introduction of 'principles of equity and fairness' and 'generally considering' further dilutes the legal standard, allowing for subjective interpretations beyond established law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-301(a) states that parties can agree to be governed by the law of a particular state. The original text fulfills this. The modified text weakens the choice of law by suggesting Minnesota law only 'generally guides' the agreement, potentially conflicting with the parties' ability to clearly define the governing law and undermining the certainty that the statute seeks to create. The introduction of 'equity and fairness' can be interpreted as overriding or supplementing established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-301 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-301 336.1-209 336.1-302 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-301 Version List Recent History 2024 336.1-301 Amended 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 2004 336.1-301 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 336.1-301 TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY; PARTIES' POWER TO CHOOSE APPLICABLE LAW. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 336.2-402 ; (2) sections 336.2A-105 and 336.2A-106 ; (3) section 336.4-102 ; (4) section 336.4A-507 ; (5) section 336.5-116 ; (6) section 336.8-110 ; (7) sections 336.9-301 through 336.9-307 ; (8) section 336.12-107 . History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 ; 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'governed by and construed in accordance with' with 'generally guided by,' and by considering 'principles of equity and fairness,' weakening the binding nature of Minnesota law. This contradicts Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-301(a), which, as stated in the Law Explanation, allows parties to agree on the governing law of a particular state. The original text clearly designates Minnesota law, while the modified text makes this designation less definitive, thereby creating a legal contradiction, making it an ambiguous legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Big Sky and Holdings agree that, at the conclusion of their review of the Information, or within three business days of the other's request, all copies of the Information, in any form whatsoever, will be delivered by Holdings and its Representatives to Big Sky.
perturbation.changed_text: Big Sky and Holdings agree that, ideally at the conclusion of their review of the Information, or possibly within a reasonable timeframe of the other's request, copies of the Information will be addressed by Holdings and its Representatives to Big Sky.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces "at the conclusion of their review of the Information, or within three business days of the other's request" with "ideally at the conclusion of their review of the Information, or possibly within a reasonable timeframe of the other's request". "Ideally" and "possibly within a reasonable timeframe" makes the deadline subjective. Also, replacing "will be delivered" with "will be addressed" weakens the obligation to return the information, introducing ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 325C.02(a)(1) refers to misappropriation of trade secrets, which is defined as disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent. This depends on the information being protected. If the information is protected, the ambiguous language regarding the return of information could weaken the protection of trade secrets. If not returned, and used without permission, the new text might make it more difficult to legally argue that misappropriation occurred due to the lack of a clear, enforceable deadline for the information's return.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the return of information, weakening the protection of trade secrets. The original text specifies a clear deadline, whereas the altered text uses terms like "ideally" and "possibly within a reasonable timeframe," making the obligation subjective and harder to enforce. The Law Explanation says that the lack of a clear, enforceable deadline for the information's return could make it more difficult to legally argue that misappropriation occurred. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Minnesota Statute 325C.02(a), states that actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. The ambiguous language in the changed text undermines the ability to prove misappropriation, as it creates uncertainty about when the information should have been returned.
